Author: S, Suhasini T; Sharma, Manisha; Mishra, Deviprasad
Title: A Survey on Deepfake Detection Techniques for Watermarking and Digital Forensics Using Deep Learning
Publication Title: 2025 International Conference on Intelligent and Secure Engineering Solutions (CISES)
Abstract Note: The rapid development of deepfake technology has introduced new challenges to content authenticity, especially in legal and forensic domains where the integrity of digital media is paramount. Deep fakes have further complicated watermarking efforts, making it difficult to assert media ownership or validate tamper resistance. This survey aims to examine and categorize existing deepfake detection techniques from a deep learning perspective, with an emphasis on their integration into digital watermarking and forensic applications. A comprehensive literature review is conducted to explore deep learning-based detection methods such as CNNs, RNNs, attention mechanisms, and facial geometry models. The study also investigates how these techniques can support robust watermarking strategies and forensic traceability. The review reveals that hybrid architectures such as ResNet-Swish-BiLSTM and spatiotemporal attention networks achieve high accuracy in detecting manipulated media. Additionally, deep learning is shown to enhance the effectiveness of watermarking and forensic workflows by enabling tamper detection, content attribution, and source verification. Deep learning-driven deepfake detection offers promising solutions not only for real-time content authentication but also for long-term forensic verification and digital watermarking. Future work should focus on building integrated frameworks that combine detection, provenance tracking, and legal admissibility.

Title: Empirical Research on Facial Deepfake Detection: Taxonomy, Challenges, and Future Directions

Abstract Note: The rapid development of generative models has led to the rise of deepfakes which are incredibly realistic manipulated media that pose serious dangers to security, privacy, and public trust. This survey specifically demonstrates a detailed assessment of the existing deep fake detection methods that includes neural networks, residual networks, attention methods, transformer algorithms and hybrid methods. Each method is computed based on their strengths, weaknesses, dataset and performance of the benchmark evaluations. Further, the comparative analysis comprises the trade-off between different methods with respect to accuracy, interpretability, computational efficiency and scalability. Thus, the evaluation signifies the existing challenges of generalizability to unseen manipulations and robustness under various conditions such as compression, noise and low-resolution media. Finally, the survey identifies gaps in research, absence of scalable and efficient systems suitable for real-time applications. Then, based on the observed gaps in current approaches and research evidence, the future directions for the deepfake detection systems determines that lightweight hybrid transformer-based approaches that integrate spatial, temporal, and frequency-domain characteristics are required to improve the accuracy, efficiency, and robustness through flexibility for practical deployment in diverse and dynamic deployments.

Author: Singh, Kaushal; Sanghani, Parag; Shah, Niraj
Title: Implementation of Machine Learning Techniques for Real-Time Deep Fake Voice Detection
Publication Title: 2025 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Vision (AIMV)
Abstract Note: Growing numbers of audio material made by computers, also called "deep fake voices," are very dangerous to people's privacy, the security of information, and computers. Aiming to correctly identify fake voice changes, this study demonstrates and analyzes real-time machine learning (ML) methods. Specifically, a special dataset with 15,000 audio clips (50 percent real and 50 percent fake) was created and used for testing. Multiple machine learning models could be fully analyzed using features like Mel Frequency Cepstral Coefficients (MFCC), pitch, and spectral difference that were extracted. Random Forest (91.7%) and Support Vector Machine (SVM) algorithms were beaten by Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN), which got 96.2% accuracy. Based on real-time application tests, CNN recognition delay was an average of 0.45 seconds per 5-second audio section, proving that it is suitable for use in real-world situations. CNN clearly did better than the other models that were tried, as shown by its positive Area Under Curve (AUC) score of 0.97 in a Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) analysis. With precision and memory rates of 95.4% and 94.8%, respectively, CNN's better performance and stability are further demonstrated. When compared to high recognition rates, the model had a 3.8% false-positive rate, showing that it needs to be improved. Results show that CNN-based machine learning models can be useful for fighting deep fake audio risks, which could lead to better real-time security solutions. For better precision and fewer false findings, it is suggested that future study combine multi-modal data analysis with advanced signal processing methods.

Author: Geethanjali, T M; Darshan, T S; Surya, K; Rahul, H U; Sheety, Ipshika N
Title: Detectify : Image Tampering Detection using Error Level Analysis (ELA) and Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)

Abstract Note: In the evolving digital landscape, the proliferation of manipulated images poses a significant challenge to the authenticity and integrity of visual content. This project investigates cutting-edge image manipulation detection techniques, employing a combination of Error Level Analysis (ELA) and Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) for robust prediction. Focusing on the widely-used CASIA V2.0 dataset, the study provides a comprehensive evaluation of image manipulation methods. Error Level Analysis is utilized as a forensic tool to identify alterations in the compression levels of manipulated images. By scrutinizing variations in error levels, the project aims to enhance the detection accuracy of manipulated regions within visual content. The CNN model is meticulously crafted and trained using preprocessed ELA images to acquire nuanced features essential for discerning tampering- induced alterations. The proposed hybrid approach, integrating ELA and CNN, establishes a robust framework for detecting image manipulation that is adaptable and efficient. Through the meticulous examination of the CASIA V2.0 dataset, this project contributes to ongoing efforts in combating digital image manipulation. This study serves as a valuable resource for forensic analysts, researchers, and practitioners working towards ensuring the veracity of digital images, offering a nuanced understanding of image manipulation techniques in the contemporary digital era.

Author: Katamneni, Vinaya Sree; Rattani, Ajita
Title: MIS-AVoiDD: Modality Invariant and Specific Representation for Audio-Visual Deepfake Detection
Publication Title: 2023 International Conference on Machine Learning and Applications (ICMLA)
Abstract Note: Deepfakes are synthetic media generated using deep generative algorithms and have posed a severe societal and political threat. Apart from facial manipulation and synthetic voice, recently, a novel kind of deepfakes has emerged with either audio or visual modalities manipulated. In this regard, a new generation of multimodal audio-visual deepfake detectors is being investigated to collectively focus on audio and visual data for multimodal manipulation detection. Existing multimodal (audio-visual) deepfake detectors are often based on the fusion of the audio and visual streams from the video. Existing studies suggest that these multimodal detectors often obtain equivalent performances with unimodal audio and visual deepfake detectors. We conjecture that the heterogeneous nature of the audio and visual signals creates distributional modality gaps and poses a sig-nificant challenge to effective fusion and efficient performance. In this paper, we tackle the problem at the representation level to aid the fusion of audio and visual streams for multimodal deepfake detection. Specifically, we propose the joint use of modality (audio and visual) invariant and specific representations. This ensures that the common patterns and patterns specific to each modality representing pristine or fake content are preserved and fused for multimodal deepfake manipulation detection. Our experimental results on FakeAVCeleb and KoDF audio-visual deepfake datasets suggest the enhanced accuracy of our proposed method over SOTA unimodal and multimodal audio-visual deepfake detectors by 17.8% and 18.4%, respectively. Thus, obtaining state-of-the-art performance.

Author: PushpaLakshmi, R.
Title: DAF-Net: A Data-Analytics-Driven Adaptive Framework for Deepfake Detection Using Hybrid Deep Learning Models
Publication Title: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Advances in Computing Research On Science Engineering and Technology (ACROSET)
Abstract Note: The emergence of deepfake technology, which is driven by sophisticated Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs), has presented a significant cybersecurity threat. Cybercrimes including identity theft, social engineering, and disinformation now use deepfakes, which are artificially modified videos, pictures, or sounds. Because these synthetic dangers are constantly changing, traditional detection techniques sometimes fall short. The unique multi-modal system DAF-Net (Data-Analytics-Driven Adaptive Framework for Deepfake Detection), which integrates contextual data analytics and deep learning, is proposed in this research. DAF-Net combines a Transformer encoder for temporal inconsistencies, a Convolutional Neural Network (EfficientNet) for spatial features, and Wav2Vec 2.0 for audio-based manipulation detection. Together, these models provide highly accurate deepfake detection across modalities. One of DAF-Net's unique features is its Data Analytics and Visualisation Module, which assigns a Contextual Risk Score and identifies spreading trends using metadata. Tested on datasets like FaceForensics++, DFDC, and ASVspoof 2021, DAF-Net outperformed current techniques with an accuracy of 93.6% for video and 91.2% for audio deepfakes. 87% of high-impact threats have their priorities accurately determined by risk scoring. DAF-Net provides a scalable, intelligent defence solution for practical cybersecurity applications by fusing data analytics and adaptive deep learning.

Author: Sharma, Vishal Kumar; Rawat, Seema
Title: Enhancing Deepfake Detection Through Dynamics of Facial Expressions
Publication Title: 2025 6th International Conference on Intelligent Communication Technologies and Virtual Mobile Networks (ICICV)
Abstract Note: The rapid evolution of deepfake technology poses significant threats to digital security, media integrity, and public trust, necessitating the development of robust detection frame-works. Traditional deepfake detection methods primarily rely on pixel inconsistencies, frequency-domain analysis, or handcrafted features, but these approaches are increasingly vulnerable to advanced generative models that produce high-fidelity manipulations. In this study, we introduce MADDM, a Masked Autoencoder-based deepfake detection model that leverages facial expression dynamics to identify inconsistencies in muscle coordination—an aspect that remains challenging for deepfake generators to replicate accurately. Our model is trained in a self-supervised manner, first learning natural facial expressions from real datasets and then detecting anomalies in synthetic videos by reconstructing masked facial regions. Evaluations on Celeb-DF, DFDC, and FaceForensics++ datasets demonstrate that MADDM significantly outperforms existing detection methods, achieving an average accuracy of 81.1%, with state-of-the-art performance on Celeb-DF (86.3%). Further analysis through intra-dataset and cross-dataset testing confirms the model’s superior generalization capabilities. The results highlight the potential of expression-based deepfake detection as a powerful and scalable solution for digital forensics and misinformation control. Future research should explore real-time implementation, transformer-based optimizations, and adversarial training strategies to enhance detection efficiency against evolving deepfake techniques.

Author: Murugan, Muthu Aravind; Mathu, T.; Priya, S Jeba
Title: Detecting Deepfake Videos using Face Recognition and Neural Networks

Abstract Note: Deepfake videos created using advanced artificial intelligence techniques, pose a significant threat to digital media credibility. This project introduces a holistic strategy for identifying these videos, incorporating face recognition, feature extraction, and an innovative deep-learning model. The methodology involves pre-processing video data, extracting facial features using the face recognition library, and training a neural network model on processed face-only videos. The project filters videos based on frame count, extracts face, and creates a curated dataset for the detection model. Face-only videos are loaded and pre-processed to train a custom neural network model, which combines a pre-trained ResNext CNN with an LSTM layer for temporal feature extraction. The model is trained using Adam optimization with a cross-entropy loss function, and after completion, it has an accuracy of 95% and is capable of differentiate between fake and real videos using a confidence score.

Author: Jalleli, Oussama; Zhu, Yi; Falk, Tiago H.
Title: Audio-Visual Cross-Attention for Improved Deepfake Video Detection and Forgery Localization
Publication Title: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Systems, Man, and Cybernetics (SMC)
Abstract Note: With the emergence of multi-modal generative models, synthesized videos are becoming increasingly realistic, making the detection of deepfakes extremely challenging. While several video deepfake detection models have shown promising performance, their focus has been primarily on the visual modality. To overcome this limitation, we propose a dualstream framework that fuses visual and auditory information via cross-attention computed between embeddings extracted from pre-trained video and audio encoders. Additionally, we design a weakly-supervised forgery localization head that infers frame-level forgery scores from coarse segment-level labels, minimizing the need for fine-grained annotations and allowing for forgery location characterization. In this paper, we describe our preliminary results showing the proposed model outperforming state-of-the-art detectors on both frame-level localization and sequence-level deepfake detection tasks. Ongoing work focuses on investigating the complementarity between the visual and auditory modalities to improve model robustness and explainability.

Title: Interpretable Deepfake Detection: Enhancing Real vs. Fake Face Classification with Explainable AI and Transfer Learning
Publication Title: 2025 International Conference on Intelligent Innovations in Engineering and Technology (ICIIET)
Abstract Note: Deepfake detection is a vital challenge in the age of cognitive computing because of its security, misinformation, and identity fraud implications. Article presents an interpretable deepfake detection model built on the DenseNet121 architecture to categorize real and fake faces. The model is trained on a 140,000 images dataset with data augmentation and transfer learning method. Optimized training methods-e.g., early stopping, adaptive learning rate planning, and checkpointing-guarantee effective convergence and overfitting prevention. For added transparency, several Explainable AI (XAI) methods, namely Integrated Gradients, Grad-CAM, Occlusion Sensitivity, Vanilla Gradients, and LIME, are combined to visualize and explain model decisions. These explainability tools demonstrate the network's attention to semantically useful facial areas, building model trustworthiness and diagnostic understanding. Experimental outcomes reveal a 98% classification accuracy, which surpasses baseline models and verifies the reliability of the proposed approach. This article contributes to the development of explainable, high-accuracy AI systems for deepfake detection, which supports the significance of interpretability in security-critical applications

Author: Sharma, Soumya; Gupta, Keshav
Title: DeepShield: AI-Powered Deepfake Detection
Publication Title: 2025 5th International Conference on Intelligent Technologies (CONIT)
Abstract Note: The growing pervasiveness of deepfake technology is enormous in its threats to digital integrity, cybersecurity, and the diffusion of misinformation. Public opinion can be manipulated by deepfake media, identity authentication can be challenged, and digital trust can be harmed. To curb such threats, we introduce DeepShield, a new AI-powered deepfake detection system that combines spatial and temporal analysis for strong forgery detection. Our approach integrates a ResNeXt-based Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for extracting spatial features and a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network for detecting temporal abnormalities between video frames. DeepShield is trained on benchmark datasets such as FaceForensics++ and DFDC and reaches an accuracy of \mathbf8 1. 9 7 % with \mathbf8 3. 4 % recall and \mathbf8 0. 4 % F1-score, all while having good generalization. Though not surpassing state-of-the-art models in bare accuracy, DeepShield prioritizes scalability, robustness against unseen manipulations, and deployability. We also provide future directions such as transformer-based architecture and multimodal detection, thus positioning DeepShield as a step towards robust real-time video authentication in digital forensics and cybersecurity.

Author: Mohzary, Muhammad; Almalki, Khalid J; Choi, Baek-Young; Song, Sejun
Title: MobiDeep: Mobile DeepFake Detection through Machine Learning-based Corneal-Specular Backscattering
Publication Title: 2023 IEEE 20th Consumer Communications & Networking Conference (CCNC)
Abstract Note: DeepFake has accomplished notable advancement with the AI-leveraged production and manipulation techniques of fictitious human facial images. Despite many benign and fun applications, the generated fake images can negatively influence the authenticity of online information by originating deception, manipulation, persecution, and seduction, defying societal quality and human rights, which becomes critical security and privacy threat in social networks. Hence, real-time DeepFake detection and limitation technologies on the mobile platform are essential to building a controlled, harmless DeepFake ecosystem. This paper presents a real-time, cloudless, lightweight mobile app for human visual DeepFake detection using machine learning technologies named MobiDeep (Mobile DeepFake Detection through Machine Learning-based Corneal-Specular Backseat-tering). MobiDeep stems from a hypothesis that the existing DeepFake creation methods, including replacement, editing, and synthesis, lack the ensemble with the reflective objects. Focusing on the most reflective area of a human face, corneal-specular backscatter images of eyes, we seek the similarity and consistency with multiple surrounding environment features, including color components, shapes, and textures. We have implemented a cross-platform mobile application to evaluate the performance using various input parameters and lightweight Deep Neural Network (DNN) architectures. The empirical results show that MobiDeep achieves high accuracy (98.7%) and rapid detection speed (less than 200 ms) in detecting sophisticated DeepFake images within a subsecond.

Author: J, Edwin Joel; P, Kalyana Kumar; M, Mohamed Silar; J, Rohan
Title: Flexible Multi-Modality Deepfake Identification with Dynamic Learning and Cross-Modality Inconsistency

Abstract Note: Rapid advances in generative models, that produce hyper-realistic modifications of photos and videos, have contributed to deepfake detection more difficult. Current detection approaches are very costly, making real-time detection difficult, and they often suffer with generalization, particularly when faced with unknown deepfake techniques. An Adaptive Multi-Modality Framework is proposed, presenting a unique set of methods for reliable and effective deepfake detection in order to overcome these drawbacks. The framework incorporates Self-Supervised Patch-Based Learning for smooth detection of localized manipulations, Dynamic Discriminator Learning (DDL) in adaptively evolve against new deepfake generation techniques, and Cross-Modality Inconsistency Scoring (CMIS) to detect anomalies across spatial, temporal, and audio-visual domains. The Inconsistency Score (IS), a novel metric that measures the probability of modified material, unifies these elements. This method allows for real-time application, improves detection accuracy, and guarantees flexibility in response to new threats. The investigations reveal that the proposed method has the potential to transform fake identification techniques by surpassing state-of-the-art techniques.

Title: Hybrid Deepfake Detection System: Leveraging AlexNet and LSTM Networks for Enhanced Video Authentication
Publication Title: 2025 International Conference on Emerging Systems and Intelligent Computing (ESIC)
Abstract Note: The rapid proliferation of deepfake technology has presented significant challenges in ensuring the authenticity of digital media, posing security risks in various domains, including social media, politics, and corporate communications. This paper proposes a hybrid deepfake detection system that integrates AlexNet, a powerful convolutional neural network (CNN), and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks to enhance video authentication. The proposed system lever-ages AlexNet's strong capabilities in feature extraction to analyze spatial features within video frames, while LSTM networks process temporal sequences, capturing the dependencies across frames in video streams. By combining these models, our approach delivers an effective deepfake detection system that is robust to subtle manipulations and capable of identifying deepfakes with high precision. Experiments were conducted on publicly available datasets to evaluate the performance of the hybrid model against standalone models and other baseline approaches. The results indicate significant improvements in detection accuracy, with the hybrid system achieving a higher recall and precision rate compared to traditional CNN and RNN-based methods. Furthermore, the proposed system demonstrated resilience against a wide range of deepfake techniques, including facial reenactment and voice synchronization. This work highlights the importance of combining spatial and temporal analysis for reliable video authentication, paving the way for advanced deepfake mitigation strategies in both public and private sectors. The integration of AlexNet and LSTM networks offers a scalable solution for real-time video analysis, providing critical support in com-bating the spread of disinformation and enhancing trust in digital media.
Manual Tags: Accuracy; AlexNet; Authentication; Convolutional neural networks; Deepfake detection; Deepfakes; Feature extraction; hybrid model; Location awareness; Long short term memory; LSTM networks; Real-time systems; Reliability; Streaming media; video authentication

Author: Patil, Abhijit; Fatakdawala, Zainuddin; Hundare, Vedant; Doshi, Harsh
Title: ExposeAI: A Lightweight, Multimodal, and Explainable Framework for Efficient Deepfake Detection
Publication Title: 2025 2nd Global AI Summit - International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Emerging Technology (AI Summit)
Abstract Note: Deepfakes are a big concern because they make it impossible for people to believe what they see online. How can we know what's true anymore when anybody can make phony films that look authentic with just a smartphone app? That's why we created ExposeAI, a program that helps regular people find deepfakes without having to know a lot about computers. ExposeAI employs a cutting-edge AI technique called Vision Transformer (ViT-Base). You may think of it as a digital detective that is really adept at finding the little, almost imperceptible signs that something is phony. We trained our system with thousands of pictures from the Celeb-DF v1 dataset, and then we put it to the test with videos from the harder Celeb-DF v2 dataset. What were the results? It worked quite well, showing that it can handle both movies and pictures. But here's what makes ExposeAI different: it doesn't just tell you "this is fake" and leave you wondering why. Instead, it uses simple visual heatmaps to show you precisely what gave it away. These bright overlays show the parts of a picture or video that appear suspect. It might be anything strange about the eyes, the skin texture, or the lighting that people usually overlook. This openness isn't simply a good feature - it's vital. When individuals understand how the technology works and can see the proof for themselves, they're more inclined to trust and really utilize the tool. The creators built ExposeAI to put the power back in consumers' hands, giving them the confidence to question what they see online and make educated judgments regarding digital material validity.
Manual Tags: Deepfake Detection; Deepfakes; Digital Media Forensics; Explainable AI; Explainable AI (XAI); Lightweight CNN; Media; Metadata; Motion pictures; Multimodal Analysis; Planning; Real-time systems; Skin; Steganalysis; Transformers; Visualization

Author: Om Prakash, P.G.; Tadi, Yoga Sairam Srihari; Karumajji, Kyathisree
Title: AI-Powered Facial Analysis: Real or Fake Image Detection, Age Group Classification, Grooming Analysis and Emotion Recognition
Publication Title: 2025 2nd International Conference on Research Methodologies in Knowledge Management, Artificial Intelligence and Telecommunication Engineering (RMKMATE)
Abstract Note: The development of deepfake technology has made it extremely difficult to tell the difference between real and altered facial photos, which presents significant privacy and security risks. This project offers a multifaceted deep learning-driven solution that can classify age, identify emotions, and conduct grooming analysis in addition to detecting deepfake photos. The method achieves 93.91% success rate in deepfake identification using a model built on CNN that was trained on datasets of real and fake faces. Furthermore, an OpenCV-Caffe model categorizes people into various age groups, a Vision Transformer (ViT)-based beard recognition model achieves 99.9% accuracy, and an Xception-based emotion detection method reaches 98.65% accuracy. Users can input photographs, evaluate several faces, and generate a detailed report on age distribution, feelings beard presence and real/fake categorization using the system's real-time Streamlit user interface. This AI-powered solution addresses the growing worries about digital verification of identity and distorted media by offering a safe, effective and automated method of facial analysis.

Author: Hemalatha, S.; Sivasundaram, Malatthi; P, Nithya; K, Kalaivendhan; K, Jestin M; T, Giri
Title: Deepfake Detection Using Live Facial Analysis for Secure Folder Access via Web Interface
Publication Title: 2025 4th International Conference on Applied Artificial Intelligence and Computing (ICAAIC)
Abstract Note: This study presents a web-based secure access system that employs real-time facial analysis combined with deepfake detection to control access to private folders. The proposed framework leverages a hybrid deep learning model that integrates Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) for extracting spatial facial features with Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks, supported by Multi-task Cascaded Convolutional Networks (MTCNN), for capturing temporal consistency and detecting subtle artifacts indicative of synthetic media. This dual-architecture approach enables the system to effectively distinguish between genuine live facial inputs and deepfake forgeries in real time. A comprehensive dataset containing both real and synthetically generated facial sequences was utilized for model training and evaluation. Experimental results demonstrate that the hybrid CNN-LSTM/MTCNN model achieved an accuracy of 94.2%, significantly surpassing traditional CNN-only approaches, which attained 89% accuracy. Statistical analysis using an Independent Samples t-test confirmed the improvement's significance, with a p-value < 0.000, indicating strong reliability of the performance gain. The system's design allows seamless integration into a browser-based interface, enabling immediate access decision-making without manual verification. This approach enhances biometric authentication security by mitigating spoofing attempts, including advanced AI-driven facial forgeries. Consequently, the proposed method offers robust protection against identity theft, presenting a scalable and practical solution for secure folder access in sensitive digital environments.

Author: C, Thilagavathi; S, Harshivpranav R; C, Kishor; S, Muthuprakash; G, Vasanth
Title: Comparative Analysis of Deep Learning Architectures for Robust Deepfake Detection using Transfer Learning
Publication Title: 2025 4th International Conference on Automation, Computing and Renewable Systems (ICACRS)
Abstract Note: The technology of deepfakes poses significant threats to the digital security, the authenticity of media and population trust by creating very believable manipulated facial images and videos. With the advancement of the generative models, the existing detection techniques have been less useful, and they require robust and generalizable solutions. The paper provides a comparative study of the most notable deep learning models such as VGG19, ResNet50, XceptionNet and EfficientNet using transfer learning to successfully detect deepfakes. On a benchmark dataset (e.g. FaceForensics++ and Celeb-DF) a fine-tuning of each model is conducted using pre-trained ImageNet weights and subjected to multiple manipulation conditions. Detailed performance evaluations based on accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score and AUC metrics indicate that XceptionNet has better performance compared to the other models because it has depthwise separable convolutions and EfficientNet has a good balance between detection accuracy and efficiency. The analysis highlights the unique merits and shortcomings of each architecture and gives important observations towards the design of next generation scalable and real-time multimedia forensic systems.

Author: Rahman Chowdhury, Md Anisur; Rabbi Ahmed, Khandakar; Robin, Shahriar Alam; Nesar, Shah Tawkir; Salam, Towfika; Ahsan, Md. Naimul; Ansari, Md Eahia
Title: Deepfake Detection in MIS: Leveraging DenseNet and Multi-Scale Information for Enhanced Digital Forensics
Publication Title: 2025 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Computing, Applications and Systems (COMPAS)
Abstract Note: The artificially created media produced with the aid of artificial intelligence (AI), commonly referred to as deepfakes, are posing a serious challenge to the integrity of Management Information Systems (MIS). To achieve this, the paper proposes a new deep learning (DL) model, DGAF-Net (DenseNet + Gabor Attention Fusion Network), to enhance the accuracy of detecting such manipulated content. DGAF-Net incorporates DenseNet121, which is used to extract semantic features with the Gabor filter as a texture feature to generate this information through a Gated Attention Fusion Mechanism (GAFM). The pair of branches in this organization enables the successful detection of both local and global facial image anomalies, particularly in realistic scenarios with compression and noise. When tested on the FaceForensics++ dataset, the model attains a better accuracy of 88.33%, which is better than current baselines, such as Vision Transformer (ViT-B16) and Swin Transformer + CNN fusion models. The findings demonstrate the validity, scalability, efficiency, and generality of DGAF-Net as a solution for real-time MIS digital forensics, as well as a scalable approach to deepfake methods that are becoming increasingly advanced.

Author: Singh, Pallavi; Sasikumar, S.; Geddam, Ratna Sekhar; Tharun, K; Kavitha, M.; Bhuvaneswari, Thangavel
Title: Deepfake Forensics Leveraging MobileNetV2 for High Precision Detection
Publication Title: 2025 Multimedia University Engineering Conference (MECON)
Abstract Note: This Deepfake technology has emerged as a powerful tool for generating synthetic media, leveraging advancements in artificial intelligence and deep learning to create highly realistic fake images and videos. While deepfakes offer potential benefits in fields such as entertainment, media production, and virtual reality, they also present serious threats, including misinformation, identity fraud, and privacy violations. The ability to detect deepfake content accurately and efficiently is critical in safeguarding digital authenticity and preventing misuse. This research focuses on the development of deepfake detection models using Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and MobileNetV2. Three primary detection methods— handcrafted feature analysis, machine learning-based classification, and artifact detection—were implemented and evaluated. A dataset consisting of 20,000 images, equally split between real and fake, was used for model training and testing, and optimized using dropout regularization, learning rate tuning, and early stopping. The CNN model achieved an accuracy of 90%, while an optimized MobileNetV2 Subtraction model improved detection accuracy to 97% by reducing overfitting and refining hyperparameters. The study underscores the importance of robust deepfake detection frameworks, particularly in an era where manipulated content is becoming increasingly difficult to differentiate from real media. Future enhancements will explore real-time detection solutions and adversarial training techniques to further improve detection accuracy and resilience.

Author: Purohit, Ravindra M.; Shah, Arth J.; Patil, Hemant A.
Title: GGMDDC: An Audio Deepfake Detection Multilingual Dataset
Publication Title: 2024 Asia Pacific Signal and Information Processing Association Annual Summit and Conference (APSIPA ASC)
Abstract Note: Audio Deepfake Detection (ADD) is of critical concern due to its social relevance. The development of ADD solution faces the limitation of the low diversity of language resources and is restricted to a limited number of speakers. However, there is a need for a more reliable ADD system due to advancements in deepfake audio generation by the attackers. Most of the existing datasets for ADD task are limited to a language count, so their usage remains limited to restricted number of speakers. Also, in most of the existing datasets for ADD, the number of issue of some speakers’ real and deepfake exists, and others are unavailable. In this study, we implemented a configuring version of Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs), namely, High Fidelity-GAN, to create dataset, GAN-Guideded Multilingual Deepfake Detection Corpus(GGMDDC). We achieved Mean Openion Score (MOS) of 4.52 for the proposed system, which proved to be better than MOS of the existing systems.

Author: Sunil, K.S.; M, Gourinandhan; M, Gourinathan; Mohan, Hitha S; Roy, Rona
Title: Audiolyt: A Lightweight Real-Time Framework for Deepfake and Auto-Tuned Audio Detection
Publication Title: 2025 International Conference on Networks & Advances in Computational Technologies (NetACT)
Abstract Note: The increasing prevalence of synthetic and autotuned speech has introduced significant challenges in audio authenticity verification. This paper presents Audiolyt, a lightweight, real-time framework for detecting deepfake and auto-tuned audio samples. Leveraging handcrafted audio features such as MFCC, Chroma, and Spectral Contrast, and a densely connected neural network (DNN), Audiolyt achieves a classification accuracy of \mathbf8 6. 8 3 %. The system is trained on a composite dataset combining ASVspoof 2019, SceneFake, and custom-generated autotuned samples using legacy songs and Audacity. Audiolyt is deployed as a web application using Flask and Render, offering real-time inference with minimal latency. Experimental results show that the DNN model outperforms CNN and BiLSTM-based architectures while maintaining low computational overhead, making Audiolyt suitable for deployment in constrained or embedded environments.

Author: Soudan, Masa; Damash, Abduallah
Title: Comparative Study of Swin and Dilated Neighborhood-Attention Transformers for Deep-Fake Forensics
Publication Title: 2025 10th International Conference on Computer Science and Engineering (UBMK)
Abstract Note: The proliferation of facial deepfakes poses an escalating threat to digital trust. While transformer backbones consistently outperform convolutional networks in forgery detection, there remains no consensus on which hierarchical design best balances accuracy and efficiency. This work presents the first systematic comparison of two leading architectures—the Swin Transformer and the Dilated Neighborhood Attention Transformer (DiNAT)—evaluated under a controlled 20-epoch fine-tuning schedule with frozen backbones on two benchmarks: OpenForensics and a frame-level split of FaceForensics++. On this schedule, Swin–Large achieves 91.8% accuracy (0.918 F1) on FaceForensics++ and 89.1% accuracy on OpenForensics, outperforming the best DiNAT variant by 8.6 and 2.6 percentage points in F1, respectively. DiNAT reduces floating-point operations by approximately 12% and decreases inference latency by 3 ms (around 10% faster on a T4 GPU), demonstrating its efficiency advantage. These results highlight the trade-off between accuracy and computational efficiency, and offer insight into architecture selection for deepfake forensics.

Title: A Multimodal Deep Fake Detection Framework for Robust and Efficient Synthetic Media Identification
Publication Title: 2025 International Conference on Information, Implementation, and Innovation in Technology (I2ITCON)
Abstract Note: The fast pace of deep fake technology poses digital integrity risks by facilitating hyper-realistic synthetic media, resulting in misinformation, identity theft, and security violations. Current detection systems lack robustness, generalization, and real-time efficiency and frequently fail against adversarial attacks. This paper presents a multimodal deep fake detection framework that combines spatial, temporal, and sequential analysis through cutting-edge deep learning methods. The model utilizes adaptive preprocessing, multi-scale feature representation, and temporal self-attention mechanisms to improve detection accuracy and robustness. Hybrid architecture integrating EfficientNetV2, a lightweight 3D CNN, and Bi-LSTM with Transformer layers provides better anomaly detection along with computational efficiency. The proposed approach provides improved accuracy in detecting subtle deep fake artifacts, better robustness against changing forgery attacks, and optimized efficiency for realworld applications. Experimental results prove to be better than current alternatives, further enhancing trust in digital content and boosting defences against synthetic media threats.

Title: Adversial Learning Through GAN and Feature Extraction Through CNN for Deep Fake Detection
Publication Title: 2025 8th International Conference on Circuit, Power & Computing Technologies (ICCPCT)
Abstract Note: Deepfake technology, using deep learning, creates highly realistic yet artificial media, creating challenges for security and privacy. Author: B, Manoj; N, Jayashree; P, Niveditha; J, Tejas; Mohapatra, Tanushree
Title: Deepfake Detection Using Advanced Learning Frameworks and Benchmark Evaluation
Publication Title: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Compute, Control, Network & Photonics (ICCCNP)
Abstract Note: Recent advances in generative AI have produced highly realistic fake images and videos, exacerbating misinformation and security risks. Deepfake detection methods can be categorized into traditional ML (e.g. SVMs, decision trees with handcrafted features), supervised deep learning (CNNs, RNNs/LSTMs, EfficientNet, etc.), and self-supervised learning techniques (e.g. contrastive learning with attention mechanisms). Popular benchmarks – FaceForensics++, Celeb-DF, DFDC and others – provide large video datasets for evaluation, but they embed specific artifacts and demographic biases. In practice, detectors also face challenges of overfitting, large model size, computational cost, and dependence on extensive labeled data, all of which hinder real-time deployment. Emerging solutions aim to overcome these gaps. For example, attention-based and transformer models (often lightweight) have been used to capture subtle spatiotemporal inconsistencies, improving robustness to post-processing. Multimodal fusion (combining visual, audio, or textual cues) and domain-adaptive training have also been explored to reduce bias and enhance generalization. This survey synthesizes these trends – outlining a structured taxonomy of approaches, reviewing benchmark datasets and their limits, and highlighting challenges (generalization, bias, performance) and promising directions (attention mechanisms, multimodal and domain-adaptive learning) in deepfake detection.

Title: Deepfake Content Detection using Deep Learning Techniques

Author: P, Yuvarani; T, Maragatham; S, Gokul Kanna
Title: Hybrid Swin Transformer–EfficientNetV2 Model for Robust and Explainable Deepfake Detection
Publication Title: 2025 5th International Conference on Emerging Research in Electronics, Computer Science and Technology (ICERECT)
Abstract Note: Deepfake content poses a significant threat to the authenticity, privacy, and cybersecurity of digital multimedia, for which the literature has been seeking efficient detection frameworks with high accuracy, robustness, and scalability. Both classic CNNs and state-of-the-art transformer-based models demonstrate effectiveness for these tasks; however, CNNs suffer from a lack of generalization to unseen forgery types, while more recent transformers are hindered by their computational complexity. In this paper, we propose a hybrid framework that combines the Swin Transformer's shifted-window self-attention with optimized convolutional blocks from EfficientNetV2 to capture both hierarchical global dependencies and fine-grained local textures. By combining the preprocessing components that are augmented and scaled in a balanced manner, and by engineering temporal and contextual features such as blink deviation, motion consistency, lip-synchronization failures, and illumination quality issues, its robustness is further enhanced. Experiments on the Celeb-DF and DFDC benchmark datasets showed a detection accuracy of 94.3%, precision of 95.1%, recall of 93.8%, and F1-score of 94.4%, surpassing existing state-of-the-art baselines, including Xception and CapsuleNet. Moreover, adversarial robustness testing revealed no more than 3% accuracy degradation when under attack on the perturbed inputs, and the model achieved 22 frames per second on an RTX-class GPU, demonstrating near real-time capability. Grad-CAM and SHAP provide explainability, making the forensic process more transparent. Such a hybrid model, which constitutes an interpretable, scalable, and robust deepfake detection system, has the potential to be applied in various domains, including cybersecurity, digital forensics, and social media content validation.

Author: Reddy, Thiyyagura Jagadeesh; Ganesh, Marrey Sai; Kumar Reddy, Mandadi Hemanth; Bhandhavya, Chintharapu; Jansi, R.
Title: Deep Learning-Powered Face Detection and Recognition for Challenging Environments

Abstract Note: The increasing prevalence of surveillance systems in both public and private domains underscores the growing need for robust human face detection and recognition capabilities. This research introduces an innovative real-time framework that leverages deep learning techniques, particularly Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), to accurately detect human faces in complex images. Through a combination of image preprocessing, training using diverse dataset and classification using trained model, this system excels at identifying faces even in challenging scenarios marked by low lighting and diverse angles. Rigorous testing on publicly available dataset showcases the system's outstanding performance in terms of face detection and recognition accuracy. To validate the outstanding performance of CNN is face detection, comparison was done using other machine learning algorithms like k-NN, random forest and logistic regression. This research contributes to the advancement of facial recognition technology, offering a reliable solution for video surveillance applications. Author: K, Sekar; Vijayaraghavan, Jayashrinidhi; G, Shruthi; S, Sri Balaji; S, Ranjith Kumar; P, Subhash
Title: Hybrid-CNN Classifier for Fake News Detection: a Multimodal Defense Against Digital Misinformation
Publication Title: 2025 Second International Conference on Networks and Soft Computing (ICNSoC)
Abstract Note: The widespread circulation of false information through digital media presents a significant challenge to public confidence, societal harmony, and informed decision-making. To tackle this issue, this work proposes a dual-input fake news detection framework that leverages both textual and imagebased analysis for improved performance and trustworthiness. Central to the system is a Hybrid-CNN model that extracts features from text using convolutional layers and subsequently classifies them using Support Vector Machines (SVM) and Naive Bayes algorithms, enhancing classification precision. Alongside, a Transformer-based image analysis module is incorporated to detect manipulated or deceptive visuals frequently used in misleading content. These components operate independently, enabling accurate assessment of both text and images, thereby ensuring adaptability across content types. Offered as an online application, the platform allows users to verify the credibility of news through text or image submissions in real time. By analyzing both linguistic patterns and visual elements, the system achieves higher accuracy in identifying misinformation. Experimental results highlight the effectiveness of the proposed solution, which provides a resilient and scalable approach to addressing the proliferation of fake news in the current digital ecosystem.

Author: Srikanth, M.; Niharika, Vatala; Govardhani, Koppisetti; Lallisri, Peddireddi; Nandini, Vegesna; Kumar, Jmsv Ravi
Title: A Hybrid Intelligence Framework for EmotionAware Deepfake Detection and Misinformation Risk Reduction
Publication Title: 2025 Third International Conference on Emerging Applications of Material Science and Technology (ICEAMST)
Abstract Note: The development of deepfakes has accelerated to increase the rates of misinformation, emotional control, and loss of digital trust. The current detection systems mainly aim at seeing or hearing something incongruent and overlook the emotional aspect that compounds psychological and social damage. To fill this gap, a Hybrid Intelligence Framework of Emotion-Aware Deepfake Detection and Misinformation Risk Reduction based on deep learning, affective computing, and explainable AI is proposed in this study. The framework is a combination of spectral spatial forensic, linguistic sentiment, and physiological emotion characteristics via a transformerdirected multimodal fusion network. The hybrid CNN BiLSTM framework structures spatial forgery localizations and emotional pathway dynamics, which are optimized through adaptive gradient control and attention-guided calibration. The experimental performance on multimodal deepfake datasets shows that the system detects correctly 96.4% with 0.97 AUC and a false emotional trigger rate is reduced by 21 percent over the state-of-the-art systems, which were correspondingly statistically significant (\mathbfpłt0.05). The proposed model increases interpretability, stronghold, and ethical online management through the detection of emotionally manipulative synthetic media and the measurement of the risk of misinformation, which provides a starting point to acquire emotion-stable and reliable AI systems.

Author: P, Kumar; Ramya, S. Thanga; Paul, T Sam; S, Senthil Pandi
Title: DeepFakeShield: AI-Based Detection of Deepfake Videos and Synthetic Media
Publication Title: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent and Innovative Technologies in Computing, Electrical and Electronics (IITCEE)
Abstract Note: The advancement of deep generative models has enabled the creation of highly realistic manipulated videos, commonly referred to as deepfakes, which pose unprecedented threats to digital security, media credibility, and public trust. As these synthetic media artifacts become more convincing, traditional detection methods are proving inadequate, creating an urgent need for advanced and explainable AI-driven approaches. With generative models producing increasingly realistic manipulations, conventional detection methods are no longer sufficient. To address this challenge, we introduce DeepFakeShield, an AI-based detection framework that integrates deep learning architectures with explainability mechanisms for reliable and transparent identification of manipulated videos. Unlike conventional binary classifiers, DeepFakeShield places strong emphasis on interpretability, ensuring that outputs can be justified and trusted in legal, investigative, and journalistic contexts. By focusing on the temporal coherence of lip movements, spatial consistency of facial features, and detection of low-level artifacts, the framework provides a multi-layered defense against both lowquality and highly sophisticated deepfake attacks. Furthermore, its modular design supports adaptability across various media formats and deployment environments, ranging from digital forensic laboratories to real-time social media monitoring pipelines. The inclusion of explainability mechanisms not only improves accountability but also empowers human analysts to verify AI decisions, thereby bridging the gap between automation and expert validation. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that the system achieves high accuracy in distinguishing authentic videos from manipulated ones while maintaining computational efficiency suitable for large-scale deployment. With its modular architecture and explainabilitydriven design, DeepFakeShield provides not only automated detection but also transparent reasoning, enabling effective defense against synthetic media and contributing to the preservation of content authenticity in an era of AI-driven misinformation.

Author: Datta, Aabir; Yadav, Om Krishna; Singh, Yukti; S, Sountharrajan; M, Karthiga; E, Suganya
Title: Real-Time Face Swapping System using OpenCV

Abstract Note: Face swapping has been a thriving genre of work which primarily is associated with the replacement or substitution of one referral face on the face of the other person, be it by means of still images or in real time. Author: Zhou, Yipin; Lim, Ser-Nam
Title: Joint Audio-Visual Deepfake Detection
Publication Title: 2021 IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision (ICCV)
Abstract Note: Deepfakes ("deep learning" + "fake") are videos synthetically generated with AI algorithms. While they could be entertaining, they could also be misused for falsifying speeches and spreading misinformation. The process to create deepfakes involves both visual and auditory manipulations. Exploration on detecting visual deepfakes has produced a number of detection methods as well as datasets, while audio deepfakes (e.g. synthetic speech from text-to-speech or voice conversion systems) and the relationship between the video and audio modalities have been relatively neglected. In this work, we propose a novel visual / auditory deepfake joint detection task and show that exploiting the intrinsic synchronization between the visual and auditory modalities could benefit deepfake detection. Experiments demonstrate that the proposed joint detection framework outperforms independently trained models, and at the same time, yields superior generalization capability on unseen types of deepfakes.

Author: Kavitha, T.; Hemalatha, S.; Ajaykanna, K.; Dinesh, S.; A.Jabastin
Title: Using Machine Learning to Detect Deepfake Videos on Social Sites

Abstract Note: The use of machine-learning-based method to detect deepfake videos on social media platforms stems from the problem of increasing deepfake videos being posted on social media while concerns over misinformation, reputational damage, privacy violations, and online scams are proliferating. This work proposes new method for using machine learning to find deepfake videos, leveraging key-frame extraction in video analysis and using a recent deep learning approach, CNN and LSTM hybrid. Our approach begins with the extraction of salient frames from video for analysis, thus minimizing computational burden while retaining visual information that is salient to the identification task. The CNN discovers spatial features within frames, finding features that represent artifacts of manipulation. The LSTM analyzes video by accounting for the temporal dimension across frames examining commonalities and inconsistencies in sequences of frames and video, building specificity of the detection approach. In experimental assessment, our hybrid CNN-LSTM model outperformed the conventional approach in distinguishing between authentic and deepfaked videos using benchmark datasets. The results of the experiment suggest that spatial and temporal analysis can effectively identify deepfakes resulting in detection platform that will scale to applications on social media.

Author: Thatipamula, Ajay; Patidar, Mukesh; Gandi, Mukesh; Nandayala, Lila Shankar Naidu; Kommula, Datta Sai Krishna; Dontha, Vishnu Vardhan
Title: Deepfake Hunter: A Multimodal Detection Framework
Publication Title: 2025 IEEE Pune Section International Conference (PuneCon)
Abstract Note: Deepfakes represent a growing challenge in digital forensics, as they generate highly convincing synthetic audiovisual content using deep learning models. While these technologies have legitimate applications in media production and accessibility, their misuse for misinformation, identity manipulation, and privacy breaches raises serious concerns. This work introduces Deepfake Hunter, a systems-engineered multimodal detection framework designed to ensure reliability, interpretability, and scalable deployment. The proposed architecture integrates three complementary modules: (1) a visual encoder based on XceptionNet to extract spatial inconsistencies, (2) an audio encoder utilizing InceptionResNetV2 on Constant-Q Transform (CQT) spectrograms for spectral analysis, and (3) an attention- driven recurrent unit to capture temporal coherence. The system emphasizes modular interfaces between preprocessing, feature extraction, fusion, and classification layers, supporting deployment across both cloud and edge environments. Experiments on benchmark datasets Face Forensics++, CelebDF, DFDC, and Fake AV Cele demonstrate that Deepfake Hunter achieves superior accuracy and robustness compared with unimodal detectors, highlighting its potential as a dependable framework for real- world multimedia authenticity verification.

Author: T M, Rajesh; Dhangi, Shravankumar
Title: Detecting Deepfakes using ConvNeXt and TimeSformer
Publication Title: 2025 IEEE 6th Global Conference for Advancement in Technology (GCAT)
Abstract Note: Deepfakes, synthetic media generated using deep learning, pose serious threats to privacy, security, and public trust. Hence, suitable detection methods are required to combat them. This project aims to tackle the limitations of current methods by presenting a deep learning-based approach to detect deepfakes. Leveraging transfer learning, a pre-trained ConvNext model performs feature extraction to discern subtle, spatial artifacts within individual frames. In parallel, a TimeSformer model is employed to analyze video clips, enabling the detection of temporal inconsistencies and motion artifacts that single-frame methods miss. To provide a conclusive video verdict, clip-level predictions are aggregated, while Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping (Grad-CAM) and LIME (Local Interpretable Model-agnostic Explanations) visualizations are used to visualize the model's decision process for transparency. We enhance generalization by training on diverse datasets and fine-tuning with augmented examples. Moreover, MediaPipe framework is used for high-speed face extraction to reduce the prediction time for real-time applications. This solution contributes to the development of powerful systems for detecting manipulated media.

By leveraging the adversarial architecture of GANs, the system learns to detect subtle anomalies that indicate deepfake artifacts. The solution integrates seamlessly into existing security frameworks, providing real-time processing and a user-friendly interface for immediate verification. Extensive testing and feedback will refine the system's accuracy and robustness. The ultimate goal is to ensure secure and reliable authentication systems that can effectively mitigate deepfake-related threats, validated through metrics like detection rate, processing speed, and resilience to adversarial attacks.

The proposed model integrates the MobileNetV2 architecture with an efficient channel attention (ECA) module to enhance performance. The ECA module was integrated after the expansion layers of the MobileNetV2 network to improve the feature extraction by adjusting the channel-wise representations. This enhanced the ability of the proposed model to capture fine-grained details and subtle inconsistencies. The suggested method was assessed using the CT-GAN benchmark dataset. These results demonstrate that the proposed model can easily detect small modifications in the medical images. Title: Hybrid Deepfake Video Detection Using Xception and LSTM

Abstract Note: Deepfakes, generated through Deep Generative Models like GANs, pose significant threats to digital authenticity, privacy, and public trust. In our approach, we integrate the spatial feature extraction capabilities of the Xception CNN with the temporal learning strengths of LSTM networks. We implemented a frame-based video classification pipeline, extracting uniformly sampled frames from real and manipulated videos. These frames underwent extensive data augmentation to improve generalization and prevent overfitting. Each frame was processed using a pre-trained Xception model for deep spatial feature extraction, and the extracted features were sequentially modeled using an LSTM layer to learn temporal dynamics across frames. Our model was trained on a carefully partitioned and augmented dataset, achieving high accuracy on unseen test samples and demonstrating strong generalization across datasets. This system contributes to ongoing efforts to counter the misuse of synthetic media by enabling scalable, automated, and real-time deepfake detection across diverse media sources.

Title: A General Classification Framework for Detecting AI-Generated Voices
Publication Title: 2025 7th International Conference on Advancements in Computing (ICAC)
Abstract Note: The rapid advancement of deepfake voice synthesis technologies has led to increasing misuse cases, including impersonation, misinformation, and fraud. Although artificial intelligence (AI)-generated voices offer legitimate benefits, their misuse poses growing cybersecurity threats. This paper presents a general classification framework for detecting AI-generated voices across Sinhala, Tamil, and English languages. Publicly available datasets for multilingual deepfake voice detection, particularly in low-resource languages such as Sinhala and Tamil, remain limited, with most existing models focused primarily on English. To address this gap, an acoustic dataset covering Sinhala, Tamil, and English was created using real voice samples either recorded or sourced from public datasets and synthetic samples generated through Text-to-Speech (TTS) and Voice Conversion (VC) techniques, including tools such as Google Translate and Resemble AI. A hybrid CNN-LSTM model was developed to classify voices as real or synthetic. Mel-Frequency Cepstral Coefficients (MFCCs) were extracted and fed into separate CNNLSTM models trained for each language. The proposed system achieved over 95 % test accuracy across all three languages, demonstrating robust cross-linguistic performance. The findings offer a foundation for scalable, AI-driven deepfake voice detection frameworks adaptable to diverse linguistic and regional contexts.

Author: Rayat, Simar Singh; Thapa, Sujal; Basnet, Raj; Sharma, Vikrant; Dahiya, Susheela; Rathod, Arun Pratap Singh
Title: A Novel Multi-Layer Framework for Human and AI-Voice Detection Using Machine Learning and Network Architecture
Publication Title: 2025 14th International Conference on System Modeling & Advancement in Research Trends (SMART)
Abstract Note: The rapid advancement in the field of Artificial Intelligence has led to the development of AI-voice systems capable of delivering informative conversations, including factual, general, and even emotionally adaptive responses. However, some individuals have exploited these systems for self-serving purposes. Traditional methods lack the capability to distinguish between human and AI voices, causing a potential leading to an increase in fraudulent and unethical activities, thereby creating an urgent need for a more advanced solution. To address this limitation, we propose our framework that integrates Machine Learning techniques with an adversarial reverse engineering framework equipped with a multi-layered decision architecture incorporating feature extraction and source utility handling. The proposed framework aims to provide real-time edge processing, equipped with adversarial robustness and supporting multilingual capabilities. It handles multiple edge cases such as data cleansing and content moderation by leveraging architectures including convolutional neural networks (abbreviated as CNN), recurrent neural networks (abbreviated as RNN), Transformers, and other specific techniques. The model was evaluated under multiple edge cases, such as content and linguistic variations, adversarial and deceptive inputs, synthetic and contextual channel effects, using publicly accessible datasets such as Standard Speech & TTS, Fake-or-Real, ASVspoof 2019, and MLAAD. The proposed framework achieved an accuracy of 92.18% for classifying human and AI voices and report generation and distinctly classified other factors such as an equal error rate (abbreviated as EER) of about 6.8%, recall of around 93.16%, and F1-score around 92.84%, confirming the effectiveness of the proposed innovative solution in both detection accuracy and robustness.

Title: Deep Fake Detection: Enhancing Content Verification Using CNN-LSTM for Mitigating AI-Generated Video Threats
Publication Title: 2025 International Conference on Metaverse and Current Trends in Computing (ICMCTC)
Abstract Note: The emergence of Generative Adversarial Networks has dramatically transformed the landscape of synthetic media, enabling the creation of hyper-realistic deep fake videos that are increasingly difficult to distinguish from authentic content. This poses a growing threat, particularly across social media platforms like YouTube, Facebook, and TikTok, where misinformation can spread rapidly and influence public discourse. Traditional video analysis techniques are often inadequate in addressing the sophistication of such forgeries, highlighting the urgent need for robust detection systems. In this study, we present a deep fake detection model that effectively combines the spatial capabilities of a ResNeXt-based convolutional neural network with the temporal sensitivity of a Long Short-Term Memory network. The architecture utilizes 2048-dimensional feature vectors extracted from final pooling layers, with dropout applied to mitigate overfitting, and LSTM layers capturing sequential dependencies between video frames. By tuning the learning rate and extending the architecture with additional layers, our model demonstrated strong performance. Notably, it achieved an accuracy of 84.8%, with precision of 89.1% and F1 score of 84.5%, reflecting its reliability in distinguishing real from fake content. The system also forms the basis of a functional web prototype, offering potential real-world applications in digital forensics, content verification, and online safety. Author: Liu, Jianwei; Fang, Xinyue; Chen, Yike; Yuan, Jiantao; Yu, Guanding; Han, Jinsong
Title: Real-Time Video Forgery Detection via Vision-WiFi Silhouette Correspondence
Publication Title: IEEE Transactions on Mobile Computing
Abstract Note: For safety guard and crime prevention, video surveillance systems have been pervasively deployed in many security-critical scenarios, such as the residence, retail stores, and banks. However, these systems could be infiltrated by the adversary and the video streams would be modified or replaced, i.e., under the video forgery attack. The prevalence of Internet of Things (IoT) devices and the emergence of Deepfake-like techniques severely emphasize the vulnerability of video surveillance systems under such attacks. To secure existing surveillance systems, in this paper we propose a vision-WiFi cross-modal video forgery detection system, namely WiSil. Leveraging a theoretical model based on the principle of signal propagation, WiSil constructs wave front information of the object in the monitoring area from WiFi signals. With a well-designed deep learning network, WiSil further recovers silhouettes from the wave front information. Based on a Siamese network-based semantic feature extractor, WiSil can eventually determine whether a frame is manipulated by comparing the semantic feature vectors extracted from the video’s silhouette with those extracted from the WiFi’s silhouette. We enhance the basic version of WiSil Fang et al. 2023 by developing a model compression method and a forgery trace localization method. Extensive experiments show that WiSil achieves 95%++ accuracy in detecting tampered frames.
Manual Tags: Accuracy; Cameras; Data preprocessing; deep learning; Feature extraction; Forensics; Forgery; Real-time systems; Streaming media; video forgery detection; Video surveillance; WiFi sensing; Wireless fidelity

Author: Gadde, Abhinaya; Kishore, G.D. K.; Talari, Tejaswi; Nunna, Sai Lokesh; Nannapaneni, Reshma Chowdary; Vamsi, K.M.S. Krishna
Title: Detecting Deepfake Images: A Deep Learning Approach with Streamlit Integration
Publication Title: 2024 International Conference on Science Technology Engineering and Management (ICSTEM)
Abstract Note: According to sophisticated machine learning algorithms, deep fake technology can produce incredibly lifelike fake images and videos that may be exploited for fraud, disinformation, and public opinion manipulation. This has raised serious concerns about the technology. In response to this emerging threat, this research project proposes a deep fake detection system utilizing convolutional neural networks (CNNs) to distinguish between authentic and manipulated media. The project begins with the collection and preprocessing of a diverse dataset containing both real and fake images and videos. The dataset is then used to train a CNN model, consisting of multiple convolutional and pooling layers followed by fully connected layers, to learn discriminative features that can differentiate between genuine and manipulated media. Experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed deep fake detection system in accurately identifying fake content with high precision, recall, and accuracy. Additionally, the system is capable of processing both individual images and video streams, enabling real-time detection of deep fake content. Overall, this research contributes to the ongoing efforts to combat the proliferation of deep fake technology by providing a robust and reliable solution for detecting manipulated media, thereby safeguarding the integrity of digital content and mitigating the potential negative consequences of deep fake misuse.

Author: Liu, Yanchang; Zhao, Zehao; Lu, Hao; Zhang, Kun; Dong, Jiaqi; Tong, Jinlong
Title: Wavelet Scattering and Spectral Feature Fusion for Robust Voice Liveness Detection
Publication Title: 2025 International Conference on New Trends in Computational Intelligence (NTCI)
Abstract Note: The growing threat of audio-based spoofing, such as replay, speech synthesis, and voice conversion, has created an urgent demand for reliable voice liveness detection (VLD) in speaker verification and voice assistant systems. We propose a novel VLD method that integrates wavelet scattering transform (WST) with complementary spectral representations, including MEL spectrogram, constant-Q transform (CQT), spectral flatness, and speech energy entropy rate (SEER). These features are fused and evaluated using both lightweight and deep convolutional neural networks: a custom residual CNN, MobileNetV2, ResNet18, and ResNet34. Comprehensive experiments on the ASVspoof 2021 benchmark, covering logical access (LA), physical access (PA), and deepfake (DF) subsets, demonstrate that WST-based fusion consistently outperforms existing approaches. Our ResNet34+WSTCQT model achieves state-of-the-art results, including a perfect 0.000 EER on PA and competitive performance on DF (0.020 EER) and LA (0.041 EER). MobileNetV2 configurations also outperform strong baselines like ECAPA-TDNN and W2V2+LCNN+BLSTM. The results confirm the effectiveness and generalizability of our approach across spoofing types and model sizes. This work provides a scalable, low-latency, and accurate front-end for future real-time audio authentication systems.

Author: Gupta, Akanksha; Pandey, Dilkeshwar
Title: Deepfake Videos Generation and Detection: A Comprehensive Survey
Publication Title: 2024 IEEE International Conference on Computing, Power and Communication Technologies (IC2PCT)
Abstract Note: The rapid evolution of deepfake technology in recent years brings forth both promising opportunities and formidable challenges. This research paper conducts a thorough exploration of cutting-edge techniques in deepfake generation and detection, shedding light on the dynamic landscape of this field. Our investigation into deepfake generation includes an in-depth examination of advanced approaches such as CramerGAN, StyleGAN, Progressive Growing GAN (PGGAN), IcGAN, SaGAN, and HifaFace, among others. Each method, from the precision in facial attribute control offered by StyleGAN to the exceptional image quality attained by PGGAN, presents unique capabilities. The paper systematically assesses the effectiveness of ProGAN, Convolutional long short-term memory (LSTM) based residual network (CLRNet), AutoGAN, Octave convolution operation (OctConv), and F3-Net in identifying deepfake content. Beyond these techniques, we also delve into various emerging generation and detection strategies, providing a comprehensive overview of the diverse approaches in the ever-evolving landscape of deepfake technology.

Author: Vaghchhipawala, Kaiwan; Nadkarni, Prathamesh; Pai, Ayush; Trivedi, Aditya; Bollavarapu, Swarnalata
Title: Voices of Deception - Detecting AI-Synthesized Speech Using Spectrotemporal Features and Hybrid Learning Models
Publication Title: 2025 IEEE 6th India Council International Subsections Conference (INDISCON)
Abstract Note: With the advent of highly realistic AIsynthesized voice, the risk of deepfake voices has increased immensely, giving rise to grave concerns in domains such as fraud, impersonation, and digital trust. There is a gap in reliable, real-time detection solutions since current detection systems typically fall short when faced with a variety of accents, speaker styles, or complex synthesis approaches. This work proposes a hybrid deepfake voice detection framework that merges both machine learning and deep learning techniques with spectrotemporal features. A tailor-made dataset was collected from three significant sources- actual speech from the Speech Accent Archive, artificially created samples using Google Text-to-Speech, and cloned voices from the DEEP-VOICE dataset. Mel-Frequency Cepstral Coefficients (MFCCs) were utilized to extract relevant features from each audio sample. Two models were created and tested: a Voting Classifier that merges SVM, Random Forest, and Gradient Boosting, and a CNN-LSTM deep learning model that is capable of learning spatial and temporal speech patterns. The two models were accurate and above 95 % and were incorporated into a user-friendly Gradio web interface to test in real-time. The findings show that combining multiple approaches with robust audio features yields a scalable and efficient solution for AI-generated voice identification. The system is highly suitable for use in security, media authentication, voice-based verification, and fraud prevention online.

Author: Badawy, Wael
Title: Secure and Efficient AI Architectures for Real-Time Video Forensics
Publication Title: 2025 International Conference on Future Telecommunications and Artificial Intelligence (IC-FTAI)
Abstract Note: Real-time video manipulation detection and content authentication have emerged as critical challenges in the era of AI-generated media. The proliferation of deepfakes, adversarial attacks, and synthetic video content threatens information integrity and digital trust. In this paper, we propose VIDSEC-Net, a novel hybrid AI framework that integrates transformer-based vision encoders, adversarially trained classifiers, and spatiotemporal consistency models to detect manipulations in compressed, streamed, and archived video data. Unlike existing solutions, VIDSEC-Net is optimized for edge deployment and supports zero-trust environments through embedded cryptographic watermark validation and federated update mechanisms. We begin by formulating the mathematical problem of video tampering as a dynamic multivariate anomaly detection task across temporal frames and motion vectors. Our architecture leverages hierarchical attention across frame sequences, coupled with frequency-domain residual analysis, enabling high-precision detection even under post-processing or compression. The system is validated on multiple benchmark datasets (FaceForensics++, DeepfakeTIMIT, DFDC, and YouTube-Forensics) and demonstrates state-of-the-art performance in accuracy, F1-score, and adversarial robustness. In addition to performance metrics, we analyze VID-SEC-Net's real-time throughput on edge platforms (NVIDIA Jetson, Apple M1, Intel Movidius) and benchmark against transformer-lite and CNN baselines. Ethical implications, including potential misuse and dataset bias, are discussed, and we propose a set of deployment guidelines for privacy-preserving and trustworthy media verification. Our contributions offer a secure, scalable, and explainable AI-based solution for video forensics, setting the foundation for future research in trustaware media processing.

Author: Sato, Tohru Djunaedi; Kharis Wijaya Wong, Yoel; Arlinton, Theona; Jabar, Bakti Amirul; Moniaga, Jurike V.
Title: Artificial Intelligence Synthetic Image Detection Using Ensemble Convolutional Neural Network Model
Publication Title: 2025 7th International Conference on Cybernetics and Intelligent System (ICORIS)
Abstract Note: Artificial intelligence has advanced rapidly in recent years, particularly in image generation, producing highly realistic synthetic images that are difficult to distinguish from their real counterparts. While such technology has practical benefits, it also poses risks, including the spread of misinformation and identity theft. This study addresses these concerns by proposing an ensemble Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model for synthetic image detection. Unlike individual CNNs, the ensemble approach integrates multiple feature extraction techniques to improve robustness and accuracy. Using the CIFAKE dataset of real and AI-generated images, the ensemble CNN achieved superior performance compared to single architectures. Experimental results show that the ensemble model reached an accuracy of 98.04%, with a precision of 98% and ROC-AUC of 0.998, outperforming ResNet (96.15%), RegNet (97.05%), and DenseNet (97.48%). These findings highlight the effectiveness of ensemble learning in identifying AI-generated content and underscore its potential for strengthening digital security and safeguarding social media integrity through real-time detection systems.

In this work we investigate using five most image models which are Swin, ConvNeXt, VGG-19, BEiT and ResNet-50, which can be helpful in linear or non-linear ways for fake news detections, it can be a feature extraction process also, which might help in improving the detection performances of anti-fake news detectors and it also can be helpful to see the data easily and introduce experiments that might improve the robustness. Title: CapsNet–CNN: AI-Powered Voice Authentication System for Secure Online Banking Transactions
Publication Title: 2025 2nd International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Knowledge Discovery in Concurrent Engineering (ICECONF)
Abstract Note: There is a need to apply spoofing and malicious attack-resistant biometric authentication solutions to protect financial information retained in online banking systems. This paper presents a scheme for efficient voice authentication that utilizes a blend of CapsNets and CNNs. The scheme efficiently extracts speaker-specific audio patterns and spatial hierarchies using Mel-spectrogram representations, as opposed to sequential models. A proprietary dataset of over 15,000 utterances collected in a multitude of contexts is used for training and validation. An adversarial voiceprint perturbation module and an anti-spoofing branch are incorporated within the model in order to recognize manipulated or forged voice inputs. Experimental results outperform the LSTM and x-vector baselines in terms of improved spoof resistance, with an accuracy of 97.83%, an equal error rate of 1.78%, and a detection accuracy of 96.91%. The method is apt for use in mobile banking applications due to its real-time voice authentication speed and security.
